title: Surface-Code Thresholds and Qubit Footprints in Shuttling-Based Spin-Qubit Railways
authors: Arun John Moncy, Reza Dastbasteh, Josu Etxezarreta Martinez, Ryo Nagai, Pedro M. Crespo, Normann Mertig, Charles Smith, Ruben M. Otxoa
abstract: We present a fault-tolerant mapping of rotated surface codes onto a $2\times N$ silicon spin-qubit railway architecture, utilizing electron shuttling to resolve the wiring fan-out bottleneck. Employing circuit-level noise modeling, we evaluate threshold performances across various noise biases. We demonstrate that shuttling check qubits instead of data qubits fundamentally improves system thresholds. Crucially, under a noise model biased towards dephasing for spin-qubit shuttling, the non-CSS XZZX surface code outperforms standard CSS variants. By tailoring the topological code to this specific inherent bias, we show that the Megaquop footprint is achievable with a distance 7 code requiring a $p = 10^{-3}$ physical error rate, highlighting a pathway for substantial hardware reductions in early fault-tolerant quantum processors.
summary: This paper investigates how to implement fault-tolerant surface codes on a 1D-like 'railway' of silicon spin qubits that use electron shuttling to solve wiring constraints. It demonstrates that by moving check qubits instead of data qubits and using the XZZX code to match the inherent dephasing noise, the physical hardware required for large-scale quantum computing can be significantly reduced.
main_problem: Addressing the wiring fan-out bottleneck in large-scale 2D spin-qubit arrays by evaluating fault-tolerant surface codes on a restricted 2xN 'railway' architecture using electron shuttling.
main_result: Shuttling check qubits instead of data qubits improves thresholds, and using the XZZX surface code under dephasing-biased noise allows a distance-7 code to reach the 'Megaquop' regime with a 72% reduction in qubit footprint.
method: Circuit-level noise modeling using the STIM package and Minimum-Weight Perfect Matching (MWPM) decoding to evaluate error thresholds and logical error rates.
model_or_system: A 2xN silicon spin-qubit 'railway' architecture utilizing coherent electron shuttling, modeled with a time-dependent Hamiltonian including external magnetic fields, micromagnet gradients, and Spin-Orbit Coupling (Rashba and Dresselhaus).
key_observables: Error thresholds (physical error rate p), logical error rates, noise bias (ratio of Z to X/Y errors), and qubit footprint (hardware overhead).
important_parameters: Code distance (d=3 to 17), noise bias (eta), idling factor, and physical error rate (p = 10^-3 to 3*10^-3).
main_assumptions: Quasi-static noise limit, Pauli-twirled approximation for uncorrelated errors, and a unidirectional 'Train Schedule' for syndrome extraction.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the 2xN railway architecture schematic; Figure 2 shows a d=5 XZZX lattice; Figure 3 illustrates stabilizer extraction and hook errors; Figure 5/6 demonstrate the optimized extraction order and 'Train Schedule'.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the wiring bottleneck and railway architecture, defines the physical Hamiltonian and noise model, presents the syndrome extraction protocol (Train Schedule), compares CSS vs. XZZX codes under various noise biases, and concludes with hardware footprint analysis.
why_interesting: The paper provides deep insights into how tailoring topological error correction to the specific, asymmetric noise profiles of a physical platform (dephasing-biased transport) can drastically reduce the resource overhead for fault-tolerant quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation mapping the 2D rotated surface code onto the 2×N railway architecture. Data (black) and check (light and dark gray) qubits are designated strictly to separate, parallel linear ‘rails’. The necessary connectiv- ity for syndrome extraction and stabilizer measurement is achieved dynamically via coherent electron shuttling opera- tions along these rails. The different circles represent different quantum dot components: white circles are empty quantum dots, black circles represent data qubits, and blue and red circles are check qubits for detecting Z or X error.

FIG. 3. Syndrome extraction circuit for an XiZjZkXl stabi- lizer plaquette in the XZZX rotated surface code. The check qubit is initialized in |+⟩, accomplished by a Hadamard gate applied to |0⟩. It is sequentially entangled with the data qubits i, j, k, l using CNOT gates (for X operator of the sta- bilizer) and CZ gates (for Z operator of the stabilizer), before being measured in the X-basis. The red ‘X’ illustrates a single physical fault occurring on the check qubit mid-cycle, specif- ically between the second and third entangling operations. During the remainder of the circuit, this single fault propa- gates to the data qubits k and l, manifesting as a correlated weight-2 data error...
FIG. 2. d = 5 XZZX rotated surface code is shown here. The light and dark gray shaded faces/plaquettes corresponds to the difference in behavior of logical operators with respect to them.

FIG. 4. Three single qubit errors: two hook errors and a single qubit error on a data qubit (shown as red X’s), forming an undetectable logical error on a d = 5 CSS rotated surface code. This choice syndrome extraction order aligns the hook error along the direction of the logical X operator.

FIG. 5. The extraction order to avoid hook errors aligning with any logical operator for each plaquettes are shown here. The dark gray colored plaquettes and the light gray colored plaquettes have to have different set of extraction orders. The extraction orders in orange are suitable for all plaquettes.
